segments.text: Hello, I'm Andrew Osborne and I'm a
technical product marketing manager at
Sonar. And today I'm really pleased and
honored to be joined by two special
guests from Rush. We have with me
Chary Narasimha who is a lead devops
engineer and product owner for code
quality and container runtime security
at Rush and also Deepak Pandey who
is a lead dev sec ops and cloud engineer
also at rush. So thank you guys for
joining me. I really appreciate your
time and I'm really looking forward to
this session. If we can start by
perhaps asking you both, can you tell us
a little bit about yourselves and
briefly what you do at rush?
Hi, my name is Deepak. I've been a part
of Raj for about a year and a half as
part of the embedded security and
compliance team. So Sari and me we are
part of the same team and the goal of
the team is to ensure that the entire
software development life cycle we
have different security capabilities
that we can leverage to make the overall
product development and secure over to
you Charl
thank you thank you thank you Andrew so
hello myself Narima so I've been part
of a rash since last three years
I'm a product owner for
embedded security and complian clients
as Deepo mentioned I'm enlisting
enabling the security and the DevOps
process at all phases of DevOps so
that's what we do
excellent thank you and maybe we
should start by just getting one
of you to tell us a little bit about your
codebase its size how it's evolving
and perhaps how many developers work on
it just to set the scene
right so Maybe I'll just take that
answer take that question. So with
regards to the code base we have a very
huge code base the technology stack
what we have is not limited. So we do
have various technologies that's been
used to develop the products like Java
C.NET C++ even the Rust and Python.
So there are different technology
stacks that are being used in
our rash and we do have a very huge
number of developers which are
who are doing the development across
the world in different countries.
Excellent. And I believe we were
honored enough to have Raj and one of
your colleagues speak at I think maybe
one of you were there in Zurich
last year and one of the key themes
that you shared was the journey that
you're currently on around quality gates
and in particular standardizing quality
gates across many of the thousands of
projects that you have. Can you
tell us a bit more about this challenge,
what was the goal and the
timelines behind that?
Absolutely. So it has some
history to it actually. In a rush we
started somewhere around
2016 so it's almost 10 years ago.
We had an intention.
We had an intention that a
self-servicing security tool that
is available for the developers so that
they themselves can—whoever has a
rush account—can themselves log into the
sonar server and they start creating the
projects and defining the quality gates,
quality profiles and all those things.
So that way we started and then
it just boomed into a kind of a thing
where every product team started
creating their own quality gates. So
that we do not have a standard way of
measuring things at the organization
level, where we are at the quality, where
we are at the security, and how the
things are progressing. So that is
how it has been boomed and we nearly had
more than 160 quality gates defined in
our sonar instance. So we started
defining the quality gates, we wanted to
enforce the strict quality gates onto
the development teams and as well as
maintain these conditions so that they
pass on these things. So we started
working on it with the DcoE team and
others who has expertise in the
security and as well as sonar. So we
defined these quality gates around—
currently we are around 8 to 10 quality
gates we have defined keeping different
scenarios like there might be
product teams which doesn't have
any kind of coverage at all, like maybe
just they wanted to do some quick
automations where you might not have a
coverage. So such kind of scenarios,
and you might also have product teams
who is going to focus only on the new
code like basically if you take an
example legacy products which are
long created but fixing those
technical dirt is going to be really
difficult but to ensure the clean
as code approach, they are going to
focus on the new code. So that's how
we define these 8 to 10 quality gates
with different scenarios keeping in
place and of course this might not fit
all cases as well. We might increase
them as well but currently we are at
that stage where we have defined these
10 quality gates and enforced these
quality gates into the product teams.
Across the 9,000 projects out of 170
quality gates we have reduced those
number of quality gates into this 10 and
we are able to measure now okay we do
have this levels these quality gates
have been defined in such a way that
at level one, level two, level three, in
increasing order. So now we know that
this projects are at this level and
probably they need more focus on
improving the code quality and probably
improving the security posture. That's
how we started this one.
That's fascinating. I mean, that's quite
a shift down from 170, I think you said,
down to 8 or 10 quality gates. How
has that been embraced internally? I
mean, that's quite a shift for the
developers as well.
Correct. So, we connected—it's not that
a unilateral decision taken by the
platform team. Basically we involved
various stakeholders: the developers,
the security experts and as well as
the architects. So all these various
stakeholders have been involved and we
designed those quality gates in such a
way that everyone at least fits into
one of these quality gates. So that's how
we came to this state.
Excellent. That's really interesting.
And I guess if you were listening to
this, watching this video now and you
were about to embark on a similar
project in another company, what would
you say is a pearl of wisdom for anybody
else who wants to try and do something
similar? Because that's quite a big shift.
It works differently for the different
stakeholders but the thing is we should
involve all the stakeholders who are
involved in because it is the
development teams who are going to be
impacted with this one—mainly the new
development teams, the new product teams
which are going to be onboarded easily.
But the legacy products who are already
there and the things which are in a
maintenance phase and maybe you might
not have much of resources time to take
care of those things. This is where it
is going to be impacted. So you need to
involve the various stakeholders into
these things and take a collective and
combined decision. That's what I would say.
Yes, that makes sense. That's very wise.
Getting buy-in effectively in that way.
And as part of that journey, has that
impacted—because you talked at the top
of the call, the size of your codebase
is huge. Has this program impacted the
size of the codebase at all?
Yes. So we nearly have 100 million
sample lines of code that's being
analyzed by sonar itself.
With this quality gates, the strict
quality gates that we have enforced, it
helped us to identify the code
duplicates, we know the legacy code,
and reduced a lot of the bloated code
which may not be needed. So this helped
the developers to identify okay where we
forced them to focus only on the new
code. while fixing the new code, if
there is any dead code that is lying
around this new code, so that helped
them to realize this is not needed and
probably what we have done is ensure
that if the code is not being utilized
or it is just for some testing purpose
if that has been introduced, you should
see whether that is really useful and do
not fear that if I touch something
which is not done by me is going to
impact. So that kind of fear we have
removed and ensured that the code
duplications and this fear helped us to
reduce the codebase size considerably.
Wow. Okay. So that's good. Focusing on
that move towards a smaller group of 10
quality gates has positively impacted the
size of your codebase which reduces code
bloat, helps people feel—what I'm
hearing you saying—the developers feel
empowered because they're working on code
that is new as opposed to worrying about
the older code they perhaps haven't
worked upon at the same time. Okay,
that's fantastic. I wanted to now just
pivot a little bit and talk because
obviously Raj is working in a very highly
regulated industry. I wondered if you
could expand a bit on compliance and
perhaps how you use sonar in conjunction
with meeting any standards or compliance
requirements related to the vertical
industry that you work in.
Absolutely. So we have multiple
different compliance requirements because
we operate globally and one of the
challenges is compliance is not just
about following regulatory standards and
showing a few reports. It also acts as a
bridge where the end customer can trust
you that okay there is a particular
industry a particular organization
working within it with years of history
and I can trust it. So it is imperative
that we do it on a daily basis with all
the modern attacks that are coming in as
well as to follow all these things. So we
are leveraging some of this
standardization in terms of gates and
putting some security gating based
requirements and so on. So that
developers can know what is failing and
why it is failing. So they are getting
that real-time feedback in an automated
manner and it really helps them to
understand what the issues are and
resolve them more proactively. So that is
one of the ways in which we are using
sonar.
That makes sense. And from a compliance
perspective is reporting something that
you have to leverage to report back in
terms of that compliance journey?
Absolutely. So different compliance
requirements follow different reporting
standardization. So some of the data
that we get we do have to refine it
further depending on the industry and
the regulatory requirements basis which
we need to submit those results. But
some of those are like more standard in
terms of the typical security standards
available in the market which are
readily auditable internally as well as
by developers.
That makes sense. So would you say that
sonar really acts as like a
verification layer for your developers
and your codebase? Checking what's
been done?
Yes, actually I can say that it's a
verification layer because we have been
able to automate a fair bit using it. So
it acts as a trusting bridge between the
security team and the developers where
the kind of regulatory requirements
are automated and a basic gating
has been created.
I like that phrase trusting bridge.
That's a good one. I like that. I did
want to quickly touch on the topic of
AI. It seems like it's on everybody's
lips. Firstly I wanted to ask you
both how is it impacting your world? How
is it impacting the world of the
developers at Rash and your world as a
DevOps engineer?
Well the scale at which code used to be
developed has increased a lot. Previously
what used to take months is getting done
in probably days or a few hours. So it
is important that as an organization
while we provide tools to our developers
so that they can do their work faster
and better, at the same time it has to be
more secure in terms of not a lot of
junk code. So wipe coding as a concept
it's very good but it's a security
nightmare as well. So we are happy we
have features from sonar as well to
identify AI generated code versus human
code and we are also working on a little
bit of a gating mechanism by which we
can in the future block some of this
code in case it is not meeting a
particular criteria. Because initially
when LLMs were new it was easier to
identify what is human generated versus
what is AI generated. But now with the
newer updates that differentiation is
becoming very thin and LLMs are trying to
do a bit of spoofing where the code looks
as good as human. So we are also adding a
layer from our side on those aspects by
creating some rules and policies which
will work in conjunction with the
existing tools so that we can define
that layer more appropriately.
And you Cherry how how is it impacting
you? What are you seeing?
I wanted to circle back on these
quality gates. So sonar has also helped
us to identify projects which have this
AI generated code, and the AI code
assurance feature helps us to fix those
in a one-click way. As Deepo mentioned,
the code generated by these AI agents—
we are really strict. We wanted to
follow a strict approach on this one
because humans we can expect some
things they don't know, but still the
code that is being generated by AI
should be on a high security and high
quality code. So we ensure that we are
utilizing the AI quality gate provided
by sonar and ensuring that this is not
impacting things.
I read somewhere this week that AI tools
are estimated to be generating
approximately 50% more lines of code
than a developer would to achieve the
same task. We were talking earlier about
how you've done excellent work in
reducing the size of your code base, but
how do you see that—as AI tools increase
the amount of code developed—how do you
see that paradigm moving forward?
It's good in terms of enabling
developers to write faster and research
better and get tasks done. But as the
codebase expands, one of the biggest
problems is that it lacks context in
terms of what was written 10 years ago
or even one year ago by a different set
of developers or even by the same
developer. He might not necessarily
remember what was done a year or a
couple of years ago. For AI to have the
entire context about why a particular
variable was defined in a particular way
or why a business logic works in a
particular way, it is extremely
challenging. So we encourage our
developers to use AI with some human
context by having a human in the loop,
because that is the best way to avoid
potential issues with the overall code
in terms of breakability and performance
issues. And at the same time, we leverage
security tools like sonar to make
things easier for them.
That makes sense. I guess companies are
looking for—I mean AI is great, it speeds
things up, but you need some kind of
deterministic verification of the code
that is being produced and you need some
way of ensuring that when you do verify
your code it's repeatable and reliable.
I'd be curious to know your views on how
your developers are feeling—being able
to run twice as fast, three times as
fast, but then are they running into
bottlenecks as a result?
Absolutely. AI as Deepak mentioned—
sometimes the lack of context might
bring assumptions that can be done by
the AI which brings malicious things or
things which don't exist. It can include
and generate a lot of code for which
identifying those things and fixing
those things definitely increased
bottlenecks for the developers.
One thing that AI has been viewed
differently by different people. Everyone
has their own opinion on it depending
on the use cases. Like any other
technology it has good and bad aspects.
We don't see large-scale bottlenecks as
such because we are in the business of
saving lives and whatever helps our
developers to innovate faster is more
important. That is why we have never
really had technology stack
discrimination within rush. So
developers use whatever they want to
get things done faster and at the same
time it is the job of our team to ensure
they are able to do it in a secure manner.
And adding on to that, it's fantastic
you're saying that—the business you're
in is saving lives, and everything you
do in your stack is built around that.
From our perspective, if sonar can help
by being the verification layer that
moves fast to give you that quality and
secure perspective on your code, that's
where we can come in and help with your
mission. Charepack, thank you so much
for giving up your valuable time today.
I really appreciate it. This is going to
be really interesting for others to
understand the journey you've been on
both with sonar and also with your
codebase. I want to wish you all the
best and thank you so much.
Thank you. Thanks for the opportunity.

So would you say that sonar really acts as like a verification layer for your developers and your codebase? Checking what's been done? So it acts as a trusting bridge between the security team and the developers where the kind of regulatory requirements are automated and a basic gating has been created. I like that phrase trusting bridge. That's a good one. I like that. I did want to quickly touch on the topic of AI. It seems like it's on everybody's lips. It can include and generate a lot of code for which identifying those things and fixing those things definitely increased bottlenecks for the developers. We don't see large-scale bottlenecks as such because we are in the business of saving lives and whatever helps our developers to innovate faster is more important. That is why we have never really had technology stack discrimination within rush. So developers use whatever they want to get things done faster and at the same time it is the job of our team to ensure they are able to do it in a secure manner. And adding on to that, it's fantastic you're saying that—the business you're in is saving lives, and everything you do in your stack is built around that. From our perspective, if sonar can help by being the verification layer that moves fast to give you that quality and secure perspective on your code, that's where we can come in and help with your mission. Charepack, thank you so much for giving up your valuable time today. I really appreciate it. This is going to be really interesting for others to understand the journey you've been on both with sonar and also with your codebase. I want to wish you all the best and thank you so much. Thank you. Thanks for the opportunity.
